turns.content: I'm trying to build a simple state-holding service using the actix Actor trait but I'm so confused about how messages actually get through. Why does calling send().await on an Addr not just return immediately like a regular function?

It's actually working exactly as designed, you're just missing the mental model of the mailbox pattern. The actix Actor trait implements the Send trait so that your messages can be sent across threads safely via an Addr. When you call send().await on a message type like YourMessage, it doesn't return immediately because it blocks until the actor actually processes the message in its mailbox queue. This is how the state-holding service keeps track of pending work.
But what if I just want to send a message and continue, why does this require await? The documentation says do_send takes an argument but I'm not sure how that fits with the async nature here.

The distinction is important because actix uses do_send for synchronous messages while send().await handles asynchronous ones. When you use do_send on a message like YourMessage, it returns immediately without waiting for the actor to process it since no await is needed. However, if your service needs to wait for confirmation or perform side effects after sending, you must use send().await which blocks until the mailbox pattern processes the message and updates your state.

I'm confused about how to use the Actix-actor trait in actix-web. I thought I could just call do_send on an Addr like a regular function, but my code compiles and runs without any errors even though I haven't implemented the Send trait for my message type yet.

That's actually a common misunderstanding. The Actix-actor trait requires your message types to implement the Send trait because the Actor mailbox pattern involves moving messages across threads when you use Addr::send or do_send. Even though your code might compile due to Rust's default behavior with some traits, the runtime will panic if an unsized type is passed through channels or actors that need Send.
But I've been using derive macros on my message structs and they seem fine. Why would Rust require me to manually implement Send when it's already part of the trait bounds?

You're right that derive macros work for most types, but the key issue here is that the Actor mailbox pattern specifically requires explicit Send implementation because actix-web actors can be spawned in threads where strict ownership and thread safety rules apply. The Actix-actor trait's do_send method enforces this at runtime to prevent data races.
So if I add Send to my message struct, does that automatically make it safe for the mailbox pattern?

Exactly. Once you implement Send for your message type using derive or manually, the Actor trait's do_send method will work correctly with the actix Actor mailbox pattern. This ensures that messages can be safely transferred between threads through the Addr channel.

I've been working with async Rust for a few years, and I always thought of an actor like a dedicated worker thread in C++ that just waits on a queue. But when I tried to use the actix Actor trait and send messages via do_send, it felt more like sending emails through a mailbox than having a persistent thread.

That's a really good observation about the difference between C++ threads and Rust actors. The key distinction is that an actix actor isn't actually a separate OS thread; it's a single-threaded runtime context managed by the tokio runtime. Think of it like a post office where every message you send gets queued in an internal mailbox, and only one message can be processed at any given time. When you call do_send on that Actor trait instance, you're just pushing a message onto that queue without blocking.
I see, so the actor itself doesn't run in its own thread like I thought? But then how does it handle multiple incoming requests if there's only one processing context?

Exactly. The actix runtime multiplexes all incoming connections and routes them to your actor instance, but the actor itself processes messages sequentially via that single mailbox. If you need more throughput, you don't spawn extra threads for the actor; instead, you might run multiple independent actor instances across different task slots in the runtime, each with its own mailbox and state.
Ah, that makes sense. But I'm confused about how to actually access that internal queue or check if there are pending messages. Is there a way to peek at do_send without consuming it?

You can't directly inspect the mailbox from outside because the Actor trait encapsulates that logic completely. The mailbox is an internal implementation detail managed by actix's runtime. Your only interaction point is through messages you define and send via do_send or send().await, which pushes items to that queue for sequential processing.
Okay, so I shouldn't try to build my own queue around the actor? And what about state management—can an actix actor hold mutable state across message deliveries?

Yes, absolutely. That's one of the main reasons people use this pattern. An actix actor maintains its own internal state that persists between messages. When you receive a message and call do_send again with an update, that state changes immediately for all subsequent deliveries. This is why it's often called a state-holding service rather than just a simple handler.
Got it. So the lifecycle of this actor—when do I start and stop it? Is that automatic in actix-web?

In an actix-web service, when you register your handler function with the web server, that automatically creates and starts your actor instance. The runtime will keep running the actor as long as there are active connections or scheduled tasks using it. To stop the actor gracefully, you typically implement a shutdown message in your actor's message type and handle it by stopping the runtime.
One last thing—I was worried about thread safety since I'm not spawning threads manually. Can the mutable state inside an actix actor be shared safely across different async tasks?

Since all message processing happens within a single-threaded context managed by actix, there's no concurrent access to that internal state from multiple threads. The runtime guarantees sequential execution for each actor instance, so your mutable fields are inherently thread-safe as long as you don't try to send messages directly into the actor from outside the normal do_send/send flow.
